items.prompt_md: In the tabulated Climbing Stairs solution, `dp[i]` is computed from which two earlier entries?
options: The full array up to i-1
items.explanation_md: You can arrive at step `i` from a single step at `i-1` or a double step at `i-2`, so `dp[i] = dp[i-1] + dp[i-2]` — a fixed, two-entry lookback.

items.prompt_md: You need to count the number of distinct ways to reach position `n`, where each move advances by 1 or 2, and today's count depends only on the previous couple of counts. Which approach fits?
options: Build a 1-D dp table bottom-up, where dp[i] = dp[i-1] + dp[i-2]
Backtrack through every possible sequence of moves and count valid ones at the leaves
Sort the positions and binary search for n
Use a hash map to count how many times each position is visited
items.answer: Build a 1-D dp table bottom-up, where dp[i] = dp[i-1] + dp[i-2]
items.explanation_md: A fixed two-step lookback with overlapping subproblems is the Fibonacci-shaped DP fingerprint. The backtracking option recomputes the same subproblems exponentially many times — exactly the waste tabulation removes.

items.prompt_md: What is the time and space complexity of the tabulated Climbing Stairs solution, before any space optimization?
items.explanation_md: One pass to fill the table, one entry per index — O(n) time, O(n) space. The array can later be collapsed to two rolling variables for O(1) space.
